title: Weekly Dose of Optimism #202
gist: A weekly roundup spotlighting five distinct optimism stories: Saronic's autonomous drone boats striking Iranian targets plus a $3B Texas shipyard, Austin's Base Power and TerraFirma milestones, humanoid robot startups Walden and Sunday, Merck's first oral PCSK9 cholesterol pill Lipfendra, and Chinese open-weight AI models Kimi K3 and Thinking Machines' Inkling closing the gap on frontier closed labs.

themes.theme: Saronic autonomous shipbuilding and Port Alpha

insights.claim: Saronic's 24-foot Corsair autonomous boats were used in live combat for the first time, with three of them striking an Iranian submarine and a ship-maintenance facility at Bandar Abbas.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A four-year-old startup moving a system from prototype to live offensive military use validates autonomous naval weapons as a real defense-tech category and signals accelerating procurement demand.
insights.quote: A system built by a four-year-old startup went from prototype to a live combat mission, a month after another Corsair performed the first known rescue of downed pilots by an autonomous boat.

insights.claim: Saronic chose Brownsville, Texas for Port Alpha, a greenfield shipyard costing more than $3 billion, with a first phase of 835 acres expandable to nearly 4,400 and up to 10,000 direct jobs over the next decade.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A $3B commitment to domestic shipbuilding capacity signals a reindustrialization thesis and a large capex cycle for advanced-manufacturing suppliers and Gulf Coast labor markets.

insights.claim: Fully built out, Saronic's shipyard could reach roughly 2 million gross tons of annual shipbuilding capacity, building crewed and uncrewed military ships, container ships, ro-ro vessels, tankers, and icebreakers using the same autonomy stack as its drone boats.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Applying a single autonomy and advanced-manufacturing stack across both weapons and commercial vessels is a vertical-integration bet that could disrupt legacy shipyards if it scales.
insights.locator: The initial yard will be able to build

insights.claim: The Port Alpha shipyard was originally slated to be part of California Forever, but California lost it because local and state government failed to pass permitting legislation in time.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Permitting speed and political gridlock are now decisive competitive variables for where multibillion-dollar industrial projects land, favoring low-friction states like Texas.
insights.quote: We hope this missed opportunity serves as a wake-up call that inaction and political gridlock have real costs for all Californians
insights.locator: wait, wasn't that shipyard supposed to be

insights.claim: Base Power Company began serving Austin homeowners under a 40-megawatt residential battery agreement with city-owned Austin Energy, installing and maintaining batteries that the utility can dispatch when demand and wholesale prices spike.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A utility-partnered distributed battery fleet is a scalable virtual-power-plant model that monetizes residential storage against peak wholesale prices while providing homeowner backup.
insights.locator: Base is beginning to serve homeowners

insights.claim: TerraFirma, a construction-robotics startup founded by SpaceX veterans, announced a $100M Kleiner Perkins-led Series A to build the full technology stack for construction robots.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Large venture capital flowing into full-stack construction robotics targets one of the largest and least-efficient industries, a potentially huge productivity TAM if vertical integration works.
insights.quote: a robotic construction company that builds the full technology stack needed to deliver an order-of-magnitude improvement in one of the world's oldest, largest, most important, but least efficient industries

insights.claim: TerraFirma's cap table includes vertical-integration operators Anduril's Matt Grimm, Hadrian's Chris Power, and Base's Justin Lopas.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A cap table stacked with proven hard-tech vertical integrators is a strong signal of insider conviction in the construction-robotics thesis.
insights.locator: It seems like the TerraFirma guys also got

insights.claim: Walden Robotics came out of stealth with $300 million in seed funding at a $1.1 billion valuation, co-led by Deviation Capital and three Toyota investors, with robots already doing useful work in a North American Toyota factory.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A billion-dollar seed valuation with deployed factory robots and strategic Toyota backing marks humanoid robotics moving from demos to paying industrial deployment.

insights.claim: Walden spun out of Toyota Research Institute in January under CEO Russ Tedrake, an MIT professor who brought over people behind Diffusion Policy, Large Behavior Models, OpenVLA, and the Drake simulator, and had robots in production within two months.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Elite robotics-ML talent shipping to a factory floor in two months suggests the field's learning-based methods are now productization-ready, compressing time-to-revenue.

insights.claim: Sunday Robotics introduced ACT-2 Preview, claiming its home robot Memo can learn a new behavior from a single fine-tuning example, generalize it zero-shot to unseen real homes, and perform with a 99% success rate.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Single-example learning with claimed 99% zero-shot generalization would be a step-change toward viable consumer home robots, though it remains a preview needing verification.

insights.claim: Sunday's CEO proposed measuring robotics progress via 'Solves' that declare two boundaries, scope and adaptation cost, arguing that demos vary by setup so a 99% figure has no meaning without context.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A rigorous 'Solve' benchmark framework could become the industry standard for cutting through hype in robotics evaluation, useful for diligence on robot startups.
insights.quote: A Solve declares two boundaries: scope and adaptation cost. Without both, 99% has no context.
insights.locator: they got ahead of the slick demo criticism

insights.claim: The FDA approved Merck's Lipfendra, the first oral PCSK9 inhibitor, a once-daily pill that lowered LDL cholesterol by a placebo-adjusted 56% in broad hypercholesterolemia and 59% in the inherited form.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: An oral drug matching injectable PCSK9 efficacy removes the injection barrier that limited adoption, potentially unlocking a mass-market cardiovascular franchise for Merck.

insights.claim: Lipfendra is a macrocyclic peptide folded into a shape that survives digestion, delivering antibody-like potency in a $10.50 daily pill, built on capability from Merck's 2024 $220M biobucks deal with Unnatural Products.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Macrocyclic peptides enabling oral delivery of injection-only large molecules is a platform shift that could unlock a broad new class of pill-form biologics, a wave to watch across pharma pipelines.
insights.quote: heart attacks can now be attacked back with antibody-like potency in a $10.50 daily pill
insights.locator: What macrocyclic peptides allow you to do

themes.theme: Chinese and open-weight AI models close the frontier gap

insights.claim: Beijing-based Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, the first open model in the 3-trillion-parameter class, with 2.8 trillion total parameters, a one-million-token context window, native vision, and full weights due by July 27th.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: An open model reaching the multi-trillion-parameter frontier class pressures closed-lab pricing power and shifts value toward companies that can build on open weights.
insights.locator: Kimi and Thinky Open Up the Frontier

insights.claim: Kimi K3 uses sparse activation of 16 of 896 experts, plus Kimi Delta Attention for up to 6.3x faster million-token decoding and Attention Residuals for roughly 25% higher training efficiency at under 2% added cost, for a claimed 2.5x scaling-efficiency gain over K2.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Architectural efficiency gains at trillion-parameter scale lower the cost curve of frontier-class models, undermining the assumption that only well-capitalized closed labs can compete.

insights.claim: On its own published evaluations Kimi K3 wins individual agentic and coding tests, scoring 91.2 on BrowseComp versus Fable's 88.0 and GPT-5.6 Sol's 90.4, and 88.3 on Terminal Bench 2.1 versus 84.6, though results are company-run and need independent replication.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: An open model matching or beating top closed models on agentic and coding benchmarks, if replicated, is a competitive threat to frontier labs' moats in exactly the highest-value use cases.

insights.claim: Mira Murati's Thinking Machines Lab released its first model, Inkling, a 975-billion-parameter mixture-of-experts model with 41 billion active, multimodal text/image/audio, up to one-million-token context, under an Apache 2.0 license and fine-tunable via its Tinker platform.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: A high-profile lab shipping a permissively licensed frontier-scale model with a fine-tuning platform bets that value accrues to customizers rather than base-model owners, reshaping the AI value chain.
insights.locator: This comes on the heels of Mira Murati's

insights.claim: Open models are compounding on each other, as Inkling's architecture draws on China's DeepSeek-V3 and Thinking Machines used data generated by Moonshot's Kimi K2.5 during post-training.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Cross-pollination between open labs accelerates the whole open-weight ecosystem, a compounding dynamic that could let open models keep pace with or outrun closed labs over time.
insights.locator: This seems to be happening here

insights.claim: Weights are the valuable part of a model for anyone who wants to inspect, customize, improve, or build on it permissionlessly, even though open-weight is not the same as fully open source since neither lab released all training data and code.
insights.speaker: Packy McCormick
insights.so_what: Distinguishing open-weight from open-source clarifies where the real economic leverage sits, guiding how investors assess the defensibility and reusability of AI model assets.
insights.quote: But weights are the valuable part if you want to inspect a model, customize it, improve it, or build on it permissionlessly.
insights.locator: Open-weight is not the same thing
